modules.name: gravis._internal.conversion
documentation: A subpackage containing converison functions.

modules.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal
documentation: Internal functions used by the public conversion functions.

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal.normalize_graph_data
documentation:
Get graph data in various forms and convert it to a single, unified form (gJGF).

Parameters
----------
data : JSON string, filepath, dict, graph object
    Following types of data are recognized:

    - A JSON string in gJGF
    - A dictionary adhering to gJGF
    - A filepath to a JSON file adhering to gJGF
    - A graph object from a supported external library
    - A list of the previous types

Returns
-------
data : list
    A list of graphs in gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) without the uppermost 'graph' key

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal.is_known_graph_object
documentation: Check if the given data is a graph object from one of the supported libraries.

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal.prepare_gjgf_dict

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal.insert_graph_data

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion._internal.insert_edge_data

functions.name: gravis._internal.plotting.d3.d3
documentation:
Create an interactive graph visualization with HTML/CSS/JS based on d3.v7.js.

# Parameters

data

: The input data needs to be in a custom format called
  {ref}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <gJGF-format>`.

  It can be provided in following ways:

  - *str*: A string in gJGF, or a filepath to a text file in gJGF.
  - *dict*: A dict adhering to gJGF.
  - *graph object*: An object from a
    {ref}`supported graph library <supported-graph-libraries>`,
    which internally gets converted to gJGF.
  - *list*: Instead of a single graph, it is possible to provide a list of graphs.
    They can be all be of the same type, but a mix of different types is also accepted.
    The first graph in the list is shown after the visualization has loaded.
    The other graphs can be chosen in the data selection menu of the
    interactive visualization.

graph_height

: Height of the graph container in pixels (px).

details_height

: Height of the details container in pixels (px).

show_details

: If True, the details container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_details_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the details container.

show_menu

: If True, the menu container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_menu_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the menu container.

show_node

: If True, nodes are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

node_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node size.

node_size_data_source

: Name of the numerical node property that is used as source for node size on load.

use_node_size_normalization

: If True, node sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

node_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_drag_fix

: If True, the position of a node becomes fixed after dragging it, i.e. the
  layout algorithm does not change its position but the user can drag it again.

node_hover_neighborhood

: If True, hovering a node leads to highlighting its neighborhood which consists of
  all incident edges and adjacent nodes.

node_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering a node leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this node contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_node_image

: If True, node images are shown on load for all nodes whose image property in the metadata
  contains a valid image URL from which an image can be fetched.

node_image_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node image size.

show_node_label

: If True, node labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_node_label_border

: If True, node labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.

node_label_data_source

: Name of the node property that is used as source for node label text on load.

node_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node label size.

node_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies node label orientation.

node_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for node labels.

show_edge

: If True, edges are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

edge_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge size (=edge width).

edge_size_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge size on load.

use_edge_size_normalization

: If True, edge sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

edge_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_curvature

: A factor that modifies edge curvature, where 0.0 means straight lines.

edge_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering an edge leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this edge contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_edge_label

: If True, edge labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_edge_label_border

: If True, edge labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.

edge_label_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge label text on load.

edge_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge label size.

edge_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies edge label orientation.

edge_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for edge labels.

zoom_factor

: Factor that modifies how close the camera is to the drawing area on load.

large_graph_threshold

: Number that determines from when on a graph is considered to be large, which
  means that before visualizing it an initial layout is calculated without moving anything.

layout_algorithm_active

: If True, the layout algorithm is active on load and leads to movement, otherwise inactive.

use_many_body_force

: If True, many body force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force acts between any pair of nodes but can be restricted to only act on nodes
  within a certain distance.

many_body_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the force. It can be positive to cause attraction
  or negative (usual case) to cause repulsion between nodes.

many_body_force_theta

: Number that determines the accuracy of the Barnes–Hut approximation of the
  many-body simulation where nodes are grouped instead of treated individually
  to improve performance.

use_many_body_force_min_distance

: If True, a minimum distance between nodes is used in the many-body force calculation.
  This effectively leads to an upper bound on the strength of the force between any two
  nodes and avoids instability.

many_body_force_min_distance

: Number that determines the minimum distance between nodes over which the many-body force
  is active.

use_many_body_force_max_distance

: If True, a maximum distance between nodes is used in the many-body force calculation.
  This can improve performance but results in a more localized layout.

many_body_force_max_distance

: Number that determines the maximum distance between nodes over which the many-body force
  is active.

use_links_force

: If True, link force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force acts between pairs of nodes that are connected by an edge. It pushes them
  together or apart in order to come close to a certain distance between connected nodes.

links_force_distance

: Number that determines the preferred distance between connected nodes.

links_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the links force.

use_collision_force

: If True, collision force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force treats nodes as circles instead of points and acts on pairs of nodes that
  overlap in order to push them apart.

collision_force_radius

: Number that determines the radius of the circle around each node.

collision_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the force.

use_x_positioning_force

: If True, x-positioning force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force modifies the x position of each node towards 0.0, effectively pushing them
  towards the y-axis.

x_positioning_force_strength

: Number that indicates the strength of the force.

use_y_positioning_force

: This force modifies the y position of each node towards 0.0, effectively pushing them
  towards the x-axis.

y_positioning_force_strength

: Number that indicates the strength of the force.

use_centering_force

: This force attracts each node towards the center of the coordinate system at (0, 0)
  to keep the graph in the display area. It may lead to unexpected repulsion effects
  if all nodes are fixed and then a single one is released by dragging it.

# Returns

A {ref}`Figure <figure>` object that can be used for displaying or exporting the plot.

# References

- <https://d3js.org>
- <https://github.com/d3/d3-force>

modules.name: gravis._internal.plotting.vis
documentation: Graph visualization with HTML/CSS/JS based on vis.js.

functions.name: gravis._internal.plotting.vis.vis
documentation:
Create an interactive graph visualization with HTML/CSS/JS based on vis.js.

# Note

This function has some known limitations in comparison to {func}`d3`:

- vis.js does not support setting the opacity of nodes and edges.
  It also does not support setting arrow colors different from the edge color.
  Accordingly, this functions ignores following properties in the given data:

  - Graph metadata: `arrow_color`, `node_opacity`, `edge_opacity`
  - Node metadata: `opacity`
  - Edge metadata: `opacity`

- Node and edge labels can not be rotated.

- Node and edge label borders may not be visible if zoomed closely.

- If edges are hidden, then edge labels are hidden too.

- Multi-edges are drawn on top of each other and therefore not discernable.

- Self-loops are drawn as unusual circles that look odd.

- The initial zoom factor can not be controlled yet.

- If an image is shown inside a node, its shape is always a rectangle, even if the
  value for node shape desires another choice.

- Static image export only works in raster image formats JPG and PNG,
  not in vector image format SVG.

# Caution

There is a known bug when a node id is chosen to be "x" or "y",
which seems to clash with internal coordinate calculation.

# Parameters

data

: The input data needs to be in a custom format called
  {ref}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <gJGF-format>`.

  It can be provided in following ways:

  - *str*: A string in gJGF, or a filepath to a text file in gJGF.
  - *dict*: A dict adhering to gJGF.
  - *graph object*: An object from a
    {ref}`supported graph library <supported-graph-libraries>`,
    which internally gets converted to gJGF.
  - *list*: Instead of a single graph, it is possible to provide a list of graphs.
    They can be all be of the same type, but a mix of different types is also accepted.
    The first graph in the list is shown after the visualization has loaded.
    The other graphs can be chosen in the data selection menu of the
    interactive visualization.

graph_height

: Height of the graph container in pixels (px).

details_height

: Height of the details container in pixels (px).

show_details

: If True, the details container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_details_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the details container.

show_menu

: If True, the menu container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_menu_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the menu container.

show_node

: If True, nodes are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

node_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node size.

node_size_data_source

: Name of the numerical node property that is used as source for node size on load.

use_node_size_normalization

: If True, node sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

node_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_drag_fix

: If True, the position of a node becomes fixed after dragging it, i.e. the
  layout algorithm does not change its position but the user can drag it again.

node_hover_neighborhood

: If True, hovering a node leads to highlighting its neighborhood which consists of
  all incident edges and adjacent nodes.

node_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering a node leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this node contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_node_image

: If True, node images are shown on load for all nodes whose image property in the metadata
  contains a valid image URL from which an image can be fetched.

node_image_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node image size.

show_node_label

: If True, node labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_node_label_border

: If True, node labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.

node_label_data_source

: Name of the node property that is used as source for node label text on load.

node_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node label size.

node_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies node label orientation.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

node_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for node labels.

show_edge

: If True, edges are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

edge_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge size (=edge width).

edge_size_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge size on load.

use_edge_size_normalization

: If True, edge sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

edge_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_curvature

: A factor that modifies edge curvature, where 0.0 means straight lines.

edge_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering an edge leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this edge contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_edge_label

: If True, edge labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_edge_label_border

: If True, edge labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.

edge_label_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge label text on load.

edge_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge label size.

edge_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies edge label orientation.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

edge_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for edge labels.

zoom_factor

: Factor that modifies how close the camera is to the drawing area on load.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

large_graph_threshold

: Number that determines from when on a graph is considered to be large, which
  means that before visualizing it an initial layout is calculated without moving anything.
  Caution: This feature is currently not available.

layout_algorithm_active

: If True, the layout algorithm is active on load and leads to movement, otherwise inactive.

layout_algorithm

: Name of the used layout algorithm (vis.js term: "solver" of the "physics simulation").
  Possible values: "barnesHut", "forceAtlas2Based", "repulsion", "hierarchicalRepulsion"

gravitational_constant

: Number that determines the strength of the many-body force acting between all pairs of
  nodes. It can be positive to cause attraction or negative (usual case) to cause repulsion.
  Only active if layout_algorithm is "barnesHut" or "forceAtlas2Based".

central_gravity

: Number that determines the strength of the centering force that pulls the graph
  towards the center of the coordinate system (0,0) to keep it in the display area.

spring_length

: Number that determines the desired distance in the links force (vis.js terminology: edges
  are modeled as "springs") that acts between connected pairs of nodes.

spring_constant

: Number that determines the strength of the links force.

avoid_overlap

: Number that determines the strength of the collision force that acts between nodes
  if they come too close together.
  Only active if layout_algorithm is "barnesHut", "forceAtlas2Based" or
  "hierarchicalRepulsion".

# Returns

A {ref}`Figure <figure>` object that can be used for displaying or exporting the plot.

# References

- <https://visjs.org>
- <https://visjs.github.io/vis-network/docs/network>

modules.name: gravis._internal.conversion.convert
documentation:
This is the module {py:mod}`gravis.convert`.

It provides functions to convert graph objects from external libraries to gJGF.
Additionally, it contains functions to convert image files or image URLs to
self-contained data URLs and HTML elements. This allows to generate visualizations
with images inside nodes that do not rely on any external resources.

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion.convert.any_to_gjgf
documentation:
Convert a graph object from a supported library to gJGF.

# Parameters

graph : graph object from a supported library

# Returns

gjgf

: Dictionary adhering to
  {doc}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <../../format_specification>`
  with "graph" as top level key.

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion.convert.multiple_to_gjgf
documentation:
Convert multiple graph objects from supported libraries to gJGF.

# Parameters

graphs

: The list may contain graph objects from different libraries,
  because each of them is converted individually to gJGF with {func}`any_to_gjgf`.

# Returns

gjgf

: Dictionary adhering to
  {doc}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <../../format_specification>`
  with "graphs" as top level key

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion.convert.graphtool_to_gjgf
documentation:
Convert a graph-tool graph object to gJGF.

# Parameters

graph : graph object from graph-tool

# Returns

gjgf

: Dictionary adhering to
  {doc}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <../../format_specification>`

# Caution

0 and 0.0 values are ignored because they represent missing values in graph-tool.
This can cause problems when such values have the usual meaning of a quantity being zero.

functions.name: gravis._internal.conversion.convert.igraph_to_gjgf
documentation:
Convert an igraph graph object to gJGF.

# Parameters

graph : graph object from igraph

# Returns

gjgf

: Dictionary adhering to
  {doc}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <../../format_specification>`

modules.name: gravis._internal.utils.args
documentation: Centralized value and type checking for user-provided arguments.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.args.check_arg
documentation:
Check if a user-provided argument has a valid type and value.

# Parameters

value

: Value that a user provided for an argument.

name

: Name of the argument, used for proper Exception messages.

allowed_types : type or list of types, optional
allowed_values : list of values of any type, optional
allow_none : bool, optional

> If True, `value` can be `None` and then no type or value checks
> are performed on it.

# Raises

TypeError

: If the type of the given value is not contained in `allowed_types`.

ValueError

: If the given value is not contained in `allowed_values`.

modules.name: gravis._internal.utils
documentation:
This is the subpackage {py:mod}`gravis.utils`.

It provides functionality that is not specific for this package
and may find use in other contexts.

modules.name: gravis._internal.utils.operating_system
documentation: Centralized access to operating system functionality.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.operating_system.is_file
documentation:
Check if a file exists.

Parameters
----------
filepath : str
raise_exception : bool

Returns
-------
file_exists : bool

Raises
------
FileExistsError
    If there already is a file at the given filepath and raise_exception is True.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.operating_system.is_nonempty_file
documentation:
Check if a file exists and is non-empty.

Parameters
----------
filepath : str
raise_exception : bool

Returns
-------
nonempty_file_exists : bool

Raises
------
FileExistsError
    If there already is a nonempty file at the given filepath and raise_exception is True.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.operating_system.open_in_webbrowser
documentation:
Open the given HTML text in the default webbrowser.

A short-lived local HTTP server is created that serves HTML text, so that
it can be opened in the default webbrowser. An alternative would be to write
the HTML text to a temporary file and open this file in the webbrowser.

Parameters
----------
html_text : str
start_delay : float
    Delay between starting the server and opening the webbrowser in another thread.
    If too short, the webbrowser might send the request before the server is running.
stop_delay_fast : float
    Delay between opening the webbrowser (or a new tab) and shutting down the server
    after it received the first GET request from the browser.
stop_delay_slow : float
    Delay between opening the webbrowser (or a new tab) and shutting down the server
    even if it never receives a GET request, e.g. because the browser does not open.

References
----------
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19040055/how-do-i-shutdown-an-httpserver-from-inside-a-request-handler-in-python
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3389305/how-to-silent-quiet-httpserver-and-basichttprequesthandlers-stderr-output
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_TCP_and_UDP_port_numbers#Note

modules.name: gravis._internal.plotting.three
documentation: Graph visualization with HTML/CSS/JS based on d3-force-graph.js using three.js.

functions.name: gravis._internal.plotting.three.three
documentation:
Create an interactive graph visualization with HTML/CSS/JS based on 3d-force-graph.js.

The library 3d-force-graph.js uses three.js to create a 3d visualization in WebGL,
hence the name for this function.

# Note

This function has some known limitations in comparison to {func}`d3`:

- Nodes do not have a border. Edges do not come with labels.
  Accordingly, this function ignores following properties in the given data:

  - Graph metadata: `node_border_color`, `node_border_size`, `edge_label_color`,
    `edge_label_size`
  - Node metadata: `border_color`, `border_size`
  - Edge metadata: `label_color`, `label_size`

- Node labels can not be rotated.

- Edge labels are not available yet.

- Hovering over a node does not support neighborhood highlighting yet.

- The initial zoom factor can not be controlled yet.

- If nodes are hidden, then node labels and node images are hidden too.

- If an image is shown inside a node, no shape is drawn and therefore any
  value provided for node shape and color is ignored.

- Static image export only works in raster image formats JPG and PNG,
  not in vector image format SVG.

# Parameters

data

: The input data needs to be in a custom format called
  {ref}`gravis JSON Graph Format (gJGF) <gJGF-format>`.

  It can be provided in following ways:

  - *str*: A string in gJGF, or a filepath to a text file in gJGF.
  - *dict*: A dict adhering to gJGF.
  - *graph object*: An object from a
    {ref}`supported graph library <supported-graph-libraries>`,
    which internally gets converted to gJGF.
  - *list*: Instead of a single graph, it is possible to provide a list of graphs.
    They can be all be of the same type, but a mix of different types is also accepted.
    The first graph in the list is shown after the visualization has loaded.
    The other graphs can be chosen in the data selection menu of the
    interactive visualization.

graph_height

: Height of the graph container in pixels (px).

details_height

: Height of the details container in pixels (px).

show_details

: If True, the details container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_details_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the details container.

show_menu

: If True, the menu container is shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_menu_toggle_button

: If True, a button is shown that allows to toggle the visibility of the menu container.

show_node

: If True, nodes are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

node_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node size.

node_size_data_source

: Name of the numerical node property that is used as source for node size on load.

use_node_size_normalization

: If True, node sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

node_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for node size if node size normalization is active.

node_drag_fix

: If True, the position of a node becomes fixed after dragging it, i.e. the
  layout algorithm does not change its position but the user can drag it again.

node_hover_neighborhood

: If True, hovering a node leads to highlighting its neighborhood which consists of
  all incident edges and adjacent nodes.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

node_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering a node leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this node contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_node_image

: If True, node images are shown on load for all nodes whose image property in the metadata
  contains a valid image URL from which an image can be fetched.

node_image_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node image size.

show_node_label

: If True, node labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

show_node_label_border

: If True, node labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.

node_label_data_source

: Name of the node property that is used as source for node label text on load.

node_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies node label size.

node_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies node label orientation.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

node_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for node labels.

show_edge

: If True, edges are shown on load, otherwise hidden.

edge_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge size (=edge width).

edge_size_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge size on load.

use_edge_size_normalization

: If True, edge sizes are normalized to lie in an interval between a
  chosen min and max value.

edge_size_normalization_min

: Minimum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_size_normalization_max

: Maximum value for edge size if node size normalization is active.

edge_curvature

: A factor that modifies edge curvature, where 0.0 means straight lines.

edge_hover_tooltip

: If True, hovering an edge leads to popping up a tooltip if the hover property in the
  metadata of this edge contains a non-empty string or HTML text.

show_edge_label

: If True, edge labels are shown on load, otherwise hidden.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

show_edge_label_border

: If True, edge labels have a small border in the color of the background to better
  separate the text from other visual elements like edges or nodes.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

edge_label_data_source

: Name of the edge property that is used as source for edge label text on load.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

edge_label_size_factor

: A scaling factor that modifies edge label size.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

edge_label_rotation

: An angle that modifies edge label orientation.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

edge_label_font

: Name of the font that is used for edge labels.

zoom_factor

: Factor that modifies how close the camera is to the drawing area on load.
  Caution: This feature is currently ignored in this plot and only here for API consistency.

large_graph_threshold

: Number that determines from when on a network is considered to be large, which
  means that before visualizing it an initial layout is calculated without moving anything.

layout_algorithm_active

: If True, the layout algorithm is active on load and leads to movement, otherwise inactive.

use_many_body_force

: If True, many body force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force acts between any pair of nodes but can be restricted to only act on nodes
  within a certain distance.

many_body_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the force. It can be positive to cause attraction
  or negative (usual case) to cause repulsion between nodes.

many_body_force_theta

: Number that determines the accuracy of the Barnes–Hut approximation of the
  many-body simulation where nodes are grouped instead of treated individually
  to improve performance.

use_many_body_force_min_distance

: If True, a minimum distance between nodes is used in the many-body force calculation.
  This effectively leads to an upper bound on the strength of the force between any two
  nodes and avoids instability.

many_body_force_min_distance

: Number that determines the minimum distance between nodes over which the many-body force
  is active.

use_many_body_force_max_distance

: If True, a maximum distance between nodes is used in the many-body force calculation.
  This can improve performance but results in a more localized layout.

many_body_force_max_distance

: Number that determines the maximum distance between nodes over which the many-body force
  is active.

use_links_force

: If True, link force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force acts between pairs of nodes that are connected by an edge. It pushes them
  together or apart in order to come close to a certain distance between connected nodes.

links_force_distance

: Number that determines the preferred distance between connected nodes.

links_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the links force.

use_collision_force

: If True, collision force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force treats nodes as circles instead of points and acts on pairs of nodes that
  overlap in order to push them apart.

collision_force_radius

: Number that determines the radius of the circle around each node.

collision_force_strength

: Number that determines the strength of the force.

use_x_positioning_force

: If True, x-positioning force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force modifies the x position of each node towards 0.0, effectively pushing them
  towards the yz-plane.

x_positioning_force_strength

: Number that indicates the strength of the force.

use_y_positioning_force

: This force modifies the y position of each node towards 0.0, effectively pushing them
  towards the xz-plane.

y_positioning_force_strength

: Number that indicates the strength of the force.

use_z_positioning_force

: If True, z-positioning force is active in the layout algorithm.
  This force modifies the z position of each node towards 0.0, effectively pushing them
  towards the xy-plane.

z_positioning_force_strength

: Number that indicates the strength of the force.

use_centering_force

: This force attracts each node towards the center of the coordinate system at (0, 0, 0)
  to keep the graph in the display area. It may lead to unexpected repulsion effects
  if all nodes are fixed and then a single one is released by dragging it.

# Returns

A {ref}`Figure <figure>` object that can be used for displaying or exporting the plot.

# References

- <https://github.com/vasturiano/3d-force-graph>

modules.name: gravis
documentation:
This is the package {py:mod}`gravis`.

It provides a simple, unified, high-level API to easily
access the capabilities of multiple graph or network
plotting libraries.

modules.name: gravis._internal.utils.web
documentation: Simple and centralized access to web-related functionality.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.image_to_html_element
documentation:
Convert an image file or URL to a self-contained HTML element with base64 encoded data.

# Parameters

data

: Filepath or URL of an image.

name

: Name shown if the image can not be shown in the browser.

width

: Width of the HTML element in pixels.

height

: Height of the HTML element in pixels.

unit

: Unit for width and height parameters.

  Available options: See
  [CSS values and units](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn/CSS/Building_blocks/Values_and_units)

data_format

: By default, the format of the image data is guessed from the file or URL
  ending of the `location` argument, or if that fails, from features of the
  binary data. Alternatively, the format can be passed explicitly with this argument,
  so no potentially incorrect guessing has to be done.

# Returns

html_element

: A suitable HTML element for the image format that contains the data in form
  of a data URL.

# References

- MDN:
  [img](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/img),
  [a](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/a)

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.image_to_data_url
documentation:
Convert an image file or URL to a data URL with base64 encoded data.

# Parameters

data

: Filepath or URL of an image.

data_format

: By default, the format of the image data is guessed from the file or URL
  ending of the `location` argument, or if that fails, from features of the
  binary data. Alternatively, the format can be passed explicitly with this argument,
  so no potentially incorrect guessing has to be done.

return_data_format

: If True, the given or detected data format is returned in addition to the data url.

# Returns

data_url

: URL with image data embedded inside it in form of text that represents
  base64 encoded binary data.

# References

- Wikipedia: [data URI scheme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_URI_scheme)
- MDN: [Data URLs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Basics_of_HTTP/Data_URIs)

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.read_data
documentation: Read data from a location, which may be a filepath or an URL.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.read_binary_file
documentation: Read the contents of a binary file.

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.read_url

Send a GET request to a given URL and read the response.

References
----------

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.detect_format_from_location

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.detect_format_from_data

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.base64_text_to_data_url

functions.name: gravis._internal.utils.web.url_to_img_element
